text:
Hi Sofia! 🌿 wait — one emoji max and the sign-off already has one, so I'll drop this.

Let me give you the clean reply:

---

Hey Sofia! So glad the rest of it's thriving — a new leaf coming is a brilliant sign. Good news: a couple of yellow lower leaves on an otherwise happy monstera is usually nothing to panic about. It's often just the plant shedding its oldest leaf or two, and occasionally a hint of overwatering.

Here's the one thing to try: poke a finger into the soil. If it's damp below the surface, hold off watering and let the top 5cm dry out fully before the next drink — then water thoroughly rather than a little every few days. It usually self-corrects.

Want me to talk you through a summer watering rhythm? Happy to.

Sofia 🌿

Ahh don't panic — this actually sounds like a happy monstera! 🌿 A couple of yellow lower leaves on a plant that's otherwise thriving (new leaf on the way — lovely!) is almost always overwatering, and occasionally just the plant shedding its oldest leaf or two, which is totally normal.

Here's the one thing to try: instead of a little every few days, wait until the top 5cm of soil is properly dry before watering — usually every 7–10 days in summer — then water thoroughly and let it all drain. It should self-correct from there.

Let me know how the soil feels and I'll help you dial it in!

Sofia 🌿

text:
Hi Marcus! Snake plants are wonderfully forgiving — water only when the soil is completely dry, roughly every two to three weeks in summer and even less in winter. The quickest check: push a finger a few centimetres into the soil, and if you find any moisture at all, wait a few more days. When you do water, do it thoroughly and let it drain fully. Happy to go deeper on light and feeding whenever you like.

Sofia

note: warm and factually fine but rambles well past the 110-word cap — Sofia's essay habit in model form
text:
Hey Dan! Curling leaves on a calathea in the afternoon are almost always the plant protecting itself from bright light and dry air, and the fact that they relax again at night is genuinely a good sign — it means the plant is still responsive and doing its natural daily rhythm rather than declining. The single most useful thing you can do is move it a little further from the window so the afternoon sun never lands directly on the leaves, and then keep an eye on the humidity around it, because calatheas are rainforest floor plants and they sulk dramatically in dry rooms. A tray of pebbles with water underneath the pot works nicely, or grouping it with other plants, and misting in the morning helps too. Give that a week and watch the new leaves — they tell you the truth about how it feels. Want me to go deeper on watering?

Sofia 🌿

Sofia 🌿

Try moving it a step back from the window so the sun never hits the leaves directly. Feel free to send a photo if it's still curling in a week and I'll take a proper look.

Sofia 🌿
